records.ety: [OE. forfet crime, penalty, F. forfait crime (LL. forefactum, forifactum), prop. p. p. of forfaire to forfeit, transgress, fr. LL. forifacere, prop., to act beyond; L. foris out of doors, abroad, beyond + facere to do. See Foreign, and Fact.]

quotes: To seek arms upon people and country that never did us any forfeit.

defs: A thing forfeit or forfeited; what is or may be taken from one in requital of a misdeed committed; that which is lost, or the right to which is alienated, by a crime, offense, neglect of duty, or breach of contract; hence, a fine; a mulct; a penalty; as, he who murders pays the forfeit of his life.
quotes: Thy slanders I forgive; and therewithal Remit thy other forfeits.

defs: Something deposited and redeemable by a sportive fine; -- whence the game of forfeits.
quotes: Country dances and forfeits shortened the rest of the day.

records.ety: [F. forfait, p. p. of forfaire. See Forfeit, n.]

defs: Lost or alienated for an offense or crime; liable to penal seizure.
quotes: Thy wealth being forfeit to the state.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Forfeited; p. pr. & vb. n. Forfeiting.]

defs: To lose, or lose the right to, by some error, fault, offense, or crime; to render one's self by misdeed liable to be deprived of; to alienate the right to possess, by some neglect or crime; as, to forfeit an estate by treason; to forfeit reputation by a breach of promise; -- with to before the one acquiring what is forfeited.
quotes: [They] had forfeited their property by their crimes.
Undone and forfeited to cares forever!

defs: To be guilty of a misdeed; to be criminal; to transgress.
To fail to keep an obligation.
quotes: I will have the heart of him if he forfeit.

defs: In the condition of being forfeited; subject to alienation.
quotes: Once more I will renew His lapsd powers, though forfeite.
